questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for routing packets between networks?
options: Layer 2 (Data Link)
Layer 3 (Network)
Layer 4 (Transport)
Layer 5 (Session)
questions.explanation: Layer 3 (Network) handles logical addressing (IP) and routing decisions. Routers operate at this layer, forwarding packets based on destination IP addresses.

questions.question: Arrange the TCP three-way handshake in correct order:
questions.explanation: TCP handshake: Client sends SYN (seq=x) → Server replies SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1) → Client sends ACK (ack=y+1). Both sides now have synchronized sequence numbers.

questions.question: UDP guarantees that packets arrive in the same order they were sent.
questions.explanation: UDP is connectionless and provides no ordering guarantees. Packets may arrive out of order, duplicated, or not at all. Applications (DNS, gaming, VoIP) that use UDP handle ordering themselves if needed.

questions.question: HTTP/2 uses a single TCP connection with multiple ___ to avoid head-of-line blocking.
questions.explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple request/response pairs as independent streams over one TCP connection. Each stream has an ID, enabling concurrent transfers without opening multiple connections.

questions.question: What is the subnet mask in CIDR notation for a network that needs exactly 30 usable host addresses?
questions.code:
# Need: 30 hosts
# Formula: 2^n - 2 >= 30 (subtract network and broadcast)
# n = 5 gives 30 usable hosts (2^5 - 2 = 30)
# Subnet bits = 32 - 5 = 27
questions.explanation: 2^5 = 32 addresses, minus 2 (network + broadcast) = 30 usable hosts. 32 - 5 = /27 subnet mask (255.255.255.224).

questions.question: A client can't resolve 'api.example.com' but 'example.com' works fine. The DNS zone file has an issue:
questions.code:
example.com.    IN  A      93.184.216.34
www             IN  CNAME  example.com.
api             IN  CNAME  api-lb.us-east.elb.amazonaws.com
options: Missing trailing dot on the CNAME target — 'api-lb.us-east.elb.amazonaws.com' becomes 'api-lb.us-east.elb.amazonaws.com.example.com.'
CNAME records can't point to external domains
Missing SOA record for the api subdomain
Need an A record, not CNAME, for api subdomain
questions.explanation: In DNS zone files, names without a trailing dot are relative (appended to the zone origin). The CNAME target needs a trailing dot: 'api-lb.us-east.elb.amazonaws.com.' to be treated as absolute.

questions.question: Your application experiences intermittent 'connection reset' errors to a third-party API. Packet captures show RST packets after ~60 seconds of idle connections. What's the fix?

scenario.context: Microservice calling external payment API. Uses HTTP/1.1 keep-alive connections via a connection pool.

options: Enable TCP keepalive with interval < 60s to prevent NAT/firewall timeout
Disable connection pooling — open a new connection per request
Increase connection pool size to 200
Add retry logic with exponential backoff
questions.explanation: The RST comes from an intermediate NAT/firewall that times out idle connections at 60s. TCP keepalive probes (e.g., every 30s) keep the connection alive through stateful middleboxes. Retry logic is a band-aid, not a fix.

questions.question: What port does HTTPS use by default?
questions.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP over TLS) uses port 443 by default. HTTP uses port 80. 8080 and 8443 are common alternative/development ports.

questions.question: Match each protocol to its primary layer and function:

right: Layer 2 — resolves IP to MAC address

questions.explanation: ARP operates between L2/L3 (IP→MAC). BGP is the Internet's routing protocol (L3). TLS provides security above transport (L5-6). ICMP is IP's error/diagnostic protocol (L3).

questions.question: A TCP connection has 100ms RTT and 64KB window size. What's the maximum theoretical throughput?
questions.explanation: Throughput = 64KB / 100ms = 65,536 bytes / 0.1s = 655,360 bytes/s = ~5.24 Mbps. This is the bandwidth-delay product limit — the window can't keep the pipe full beyond this.

questions.question: Which HTTP method is idempotent?
options: POST
PUT
PATCH (without If-Match)
None of the above
questions.explanation: PUT is idempotent — sending the same PUT request multiple times produces the same result. POST creates new resources (not idempotent). GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST and PATCH generally are not.

questions.question: Users report that your website loads slowly from Asia but is fast in Europe. CDN is configured. Diagnose:

presentation.context: Global SaaS app. Cloudflare CDN configured. Origin server in Frankfurt, Germany.
findings: Cache-Control: private, no-store on all API responses

options: CDN can't cache authenticated content — 'private, no-store' headers bypass CDN
Missing PoP (Point of Presence) in Asia
DNS resolution is slow for Asian users
TCP congestion window too small for high-latency connections
questions.explanation: Cache-Control: private, no-store tells the CDN to never cache these responses. With only 12% hit ratio, almost everything goes to origin (Frankfurt). Fix: separate cacheable static assets, use 'stale-while-revalidate', or add an origin in Asia.

questions.question: Should ISPs be allowed to perform deep packet inspection (DPI) to prioritize traffic types?
questions.scenario: ISPs argue DPI helps manage network congestion (prioritizing VoIP over torrents). Critics say it violates net neutrality and user privacy by inspecting encrypted traffic metadata.

options.label: Yes — network management requires visibility into traffic patterns
No — all packets should be treated equally regardless of content
Only for anonymized/aggregated traffic management, never per-user
Users should opt-in to prioritization in exchange for lower latency

questions.explanation: Aggregate traffic management (e.g., prioritizing real-time protocols generally) can improve QoS without violating individual privacy. Per-user DPI raises serious privacy concerns and enables discriminatory practices.

questions.question: You're designing a real-time multiplayer game protocol. Which transport approach?
questions.scenario: Fast-paced FPS game. 64 players per server. Need position updates 60 times/second. Acceptable packet loss: 5%. Latency budget: <50ms.

options.label: UDP with custom reliability layer for critical packets only

questions.explanation: FPS games universally use UDP with custom reliability. Position updates are fire-and-forget (old positions are irrelevant). Critical events (kills, pickups) get application-level acknowledgment. TCP's retransmission adds unacceptable latency.
